recent_lessons: Jalen Duren REB T6 missed at 5 rebounds (1 below threshold) in only 26 minutes against MIA — MIA's elite rebounding suppression profile and low-pace scheme were not sufficiently weighted; MIA's zone-based defense limits second-chance opportunities which directly compresses big-man board totals, and the 25th-percentile L10 floor of 7 was derived from games without this specific defensive suppression context.

3PM props went 2/5 (40%) — both misses were 0 actual on T1 picks (Murray 3PM miss: 0 actual vs 1 pick; Mitchell 3PM miss: 0 actual vs 1 pick) — a T1 3PM pick with a down-trend step-down offers very limited margin, and a single cold shooting night produces a goose egg; the 8/10 and 9/10 historical hit rates may overstate floor reliability when opponent defense and game script converge against shooters.

Donovan Mitchell's PTS miss (17 actual vs 20 pick) on a B2B home game against a depleted PHI occurred despite a 7/10 hit rate and blowout-resilient offset — the blowout context likely drove bench-minute conservation in the fourth quarter, eroding what would have been an otherwise reachable floor; the blowout-resilient offset should not fully neutralize the BLOWOUT_RISK -10% on PTS props when the spread is as large as 12.5.

Jamal Murray's 3PM miss (0 actual vs 1 pick) came despite an 8/10 hit rate, up trend, and vs_tough=94% context — his ankle questionable tag was noted as informational but not penalized enough on a volatile shooting prop; even a single-3PM floor is not protected when shooting mechanics may be compromised by a soft-tissue concern.

Jaylen Brown's iron-floor PTS tag failed badly (8 actual vs 20 pick) in just 15 minutes — the post-game context shows only 15 minutes played, suggesting an early exit or coach decision. The iron-floor tag and 8/10 hit rate did not protect against a minutes collapse. When min_floor is derived from full-game samples, a 15-minute game is structurally unforeseeable, but the miss reveals that iron-floor confidence capping at 80% may be appropriate even for consistent-tagged scorers when no minute-protection signal exists.

Brandon Ingram's PTS miss (9 actual vs 15 pick, VOLATILE tag, 7/10 hit rate) in 36 minutes confirms that the VOLATILE penalty at 70-72% confidence is the correct zone for him — the reasoning cited vs_soft=85% as a supportive signal, but 7/10 at T15 for a volatile SF is a marginal pick even in favorable matchups. The Analyst should treat 7/10 VOLATILE PTS picks at T15+ as borderline-skip candidates regardless of DvP rating.

RJ Barrett's AST miss (1 actual vs 2 pick, iron-floor tag, 8/10 hit rate) in 32 minutes with a down trend is a low-tier variance miss — the player logged 32 minutes and 25 points but only 1 assist. The down trend was correctly noted but the iron-floor tag suppressed the VOLATILE penalty. Iron-floor classification should not override a down-trend + declining AST pattern for wing scorers; the trend signal deserved more weight.

Julius Randle's AST miss (2 actual vs 4 pick, 7/10 hit rate, VOLATILE) confirms that T4 AST picks for non-primary PGs remain risky at 70% confidence — the reasoning correctly flagged VOLATILE -5% bringing confidence to 70%, and the pick still missed. The system is right to be cautious here; this classification validates the 70% floor threshold as a borderline zone.

Cooper Flagg's PTS miss (14 actual vs 15 pick, iron-floor tag, 7/10 rate) with only 1 point below threshold illustrates that T15 iron-floor picks at exactly 70% confidence with FG_COLD:-18% present are genuinely marginal. The FG_COLD signal was logged as 'informational only' but a -18% shooting suppression on a 15-point threshold is a material risk that should trigger a tier step-down to T10.

LaMelo Ball's PTS miss (14 actual vs 15 pick, 9/10 rate, 22 minutes played) — the post-game context shows only 22 minutes, well below his typical load. A min_floor=22.9 below 24 triggered a confidence cap but did not trigger a tier step-down. The minutes-floor cap rule should be evaluated for whether it's doing enough: a min_floor below 24 should perhaps force a mandatory tier step-down to T10 rather than just capping confidence.
recent_reinforcements: Iron-floor REB picks went 3/3 (100%) — Kawhi REB at T4 (7 actual vs 4 pick), Mobley REB stepped to T2 (8 actual vs 4), Murray REB at T2 (8 actual vs 2); the 25th-percentile gate and iron-floor tag reliably identified safe rebound floors even in a blowout-risk CLE game.

AST floor picks at T2 and T4 for elite primary ball-handlers (SGA 15 actual vs 4 pick, Jokic 13 actual vs 6, Kawhi 8 actual vs 2, Mitchell 6 actual vs 4) were the session's most consistent prop type at 80%; low AST thresholds for high-usage hubs provide reliable value.

Kawhi Leonard's multi-prop card (PTS/REB/AST/3PM) hit 4/4 on a night he played 35 minutes with 29 pts, 7 reb, 8 ast, 2 3PM — the consistent tag and iron-floor anchors across all four props validated the multi-prop stacking approach on high-usage wings with stable role definitions.

SGA's PTS iron-floor at T25 (35 actual vs 25 pick, 78% confidence) confirmed that iron-floor elevation is justified for MVP-caliber scorers in favorable home matchups, even after applying the VOLATILE -5% penalty.

The VOLATILE -5% modifier applied to both SGA PTS (landed 78%) and Jokic AST (landed 75%) did not prevent either from hitting comfortably — both players outperformed their pick values by wide margins (35 vs 25, 13 vs 6), suggesting the VOLATILE flag is properly conservative without over-penalizing elite performers.

Low-threshold PTS floors (T10 and T15) on high-usage primaries with 9/10 or 10/10 hit rates proved near-automatic: Randle T10 (hit 14), RJ Barrett T10 (hit 25), Duren T15 (hit 26), Brandon Miller T15 (hit 23) — these tiers are the system's most reliable prop class.

Multi-stat coverage on elite scorers paid off cleanly: Durant went 3/3 (PTS/REB/AST), Doncic went 3/3 (PTS/REB/3PM), and Derrick White went 3/3 (PTS/REB/AST) — stacking confirmed players at their floor tiers across prop types is high-value.

The 3PM T1 floor (OVER 1) strategy on high-volume shooters was 5/5 when restricted to consistent-tagged players with 9/10 or 10/10 rates (Doncic, Booker, Reaves, Cunningham, Knueppel) — this tier is a systematic edge.
REB picks at T4 and T6 with confirmed L10 floor-gate passage went 8/8 across all players — the 25th-percentile 3rd-lowest gate is functioning correctly as a filter against volatility.

The blowout-resilient PTS classification correctly protected Duren (+14.5 underdog, hit 26) and Cunningham (+14.5 underdog, hit 15 AST) — the primary scorer exemption from blowout secondary-skip is validated.
recent_recommendations: Downgrade or skip 3PM T1 picks when trend=down AND opponent defense rating is tough — both Mitchell and Murray missed at 0 3PM despite strong historical hit rates; a down-trend step-down to T1 on a tight floor leaves no margin for cold-shooting variance, and tough defensive opponents further compress 3PM opportunity; consider skipping 3PM entirely under this combined condition rather than accepting T1 as a safe floor.

On PTS picks with BLOWOUT_RISK (spread_abs ≥ 10), do not let the blowout-resilient offset fully neutralize the -10% penalty — Mitchell's 17 PTS vs 20 pick illustrates that even resilient scorers get benched in fourth quarters of large blowouts; treat blowout-resilient as a -5% offset to the -10% penalty (net -5%) rather than a full zero-out, and cap PTS confidence at 74% when spread_abs ≥ 12.

When a player carries a QUESTIONABLE ankle tag, apply an additional -5% penalty to all shooting-dependent props (3PM, PTS) regardless of trend direction — Murray's ankle concern was flagged as informational only, but his 0 3PM result suggests compromised shooting mechanics; the ankle tag should function as a hard signal for shooting props, not just a minutes-floor caution.

REB and AST floor props on elite, high-usage players at T2–T4 thresholds are the session's most reliable selections — consider prioritizing these prop types over 3PM when building parlays; the 3PM category hit rate of 40% versus REB 100% and AST 80% suggests 3PM legs meaningfully increase parlay bust risk.

For Donovan Mitchell specifically, flag that his PTS pick at T20 on a B2B with spread_abs ≥ 12 is a pattern that produced a miss; before the next B2B Mitchell PTS pick in a large-spread game, verify fourth-quarter minutes history in blowouts rather than relying solely on historical hit rate against the opponent.

Institute a mandatory tier step-down for PTS picks where min_floor < 24 AND the pick tier is T15 or higher: LaMelo Ball (min_floor=22.9, T15 missed by 1) and Flagg (FG_COLD -18%, T15 missed by 1) both fell just below threshold — dropping to T10 in these cases costs little upside and eliminates the near-miss exposure. Apply this as a rule: min_floor < 24 on a T15 PTS pick → step to T10.

Treat FG_COLD >= -15% as a hard tier-reduction signal, not informational: Flagg's FG_COLD was -18% and his PTS pick was T15 at 70% confidence. The informational-only designation allowed an analytically weak pick to proceed. Recommendation: FG_COLD <= -15% should force a one-tier step-down on PTS picks, matching the existing FG_MARGIN_THIN rule logic.

For wing scorers (SG/SF) with a down trend on AST, do not apply iron-floor classification to suppress the VOLATILE penalty: Barrett's AST miss (1 actual, down trend, 32 minutes, 25 points scored) shows that high scoring output does not guarantee assist accumulation. The down trend on AST for wings should trigger a VOLATILE deduction even when the iron-floor tag is present — iron-floor should protect the tier pick, not the confidence level.

Add a minutes-exit risk modifier for players whose L10 log contains any game with fewer than 20 minutes played in a non-DNP context: Brown played only 15 minutes despite no pre-game flag. If any L10 game shows <20 active minutes (not a DNP), apply a -5% confidence penalty and note as MINUTES_RISK. This is a distinct signal from min_floor and would have reduced Brown's PTS confidence below the 80% assigned.

For 3PM picks classified as VOLATILE with a tough DvP, maintain the existing approach of not applying additional tough-DvP penalties per the 3PM noise rule — the Edwards 3PM miss (1 actual, 7/10 rate, tough DvP) is a clean variance outcome on a 70% confidence pick. The rule is correct; the Edwards miss is within expected 30% failure rate and does not warrant rule changes.
